├── boot (see below for more details)

├── cdrom

├── dev (devtmpfs)

├── etc

├── home
│   ├── user1
│   └── user2

├── lib

├── lib64

├── media

├── mnt

├── opt

├── proc (proc)

├── root

├── run (tmpfs)

├── sbin

├── snap

├── srv

├── sys (sysfs)

├── tmp (tmpfs)

├── usr

│   └── local

└── var (see below for more details)

Legend
Blue: system dataset, tagged with the com.ubuntu:type=system user property
Yellow: user dataset, tagged with the com.ubuntu:type=user user property
Grey (system or persistent, see below for details)
Green (system or persistent, per package hook, see below for details)
Red: Non zfs partition

Case of /boot:

boot on a system with an ESP

└── boot

    └── efi (vfat)

            └── EFI/ubuntu/grub (subdirectory in ESP)

/boot/grub is a bind mount at startup to /boot/efi/EFI/ubuntu/grub

boot on a system without an ESP

└── boot

    └── grub (ext4)

Case of /var:

We consider 3 categories of data in /var:

  1. System data that is part of the system state and must be kept in sync with the system like dpkg, apt, system-wide daemons data.
  2. Application data global to the system like logs, mails, spools, ...
  3. Application data managed by applications but which are not part of the system. This is typically the case of databases.

├── backups

├── cache

├── crash

├── games

├── lib

│   ├── AccountsService

│   ├── apt

│   ├── aptitude

│   ├── docker

│   ├── dpkg

│   ├── grafana

│   ├── influxdb

│   ├── jenkins

│   ├── mysql (both mysql and mariadb)

│   ├── [...]

│   ├── postgresql

│   ├── NetworkManager

│   ├── snapd

│   └── […]

├── local

├── lock -> /run/lock

├── log

├── mail

├── metrics

├── opt

├── run -> /run

├── snap

├── spool

├── tmp

└── www

Legend
Blue: system dataset, tagged with the com.ubuntu:type=system user property
Yellow: user dataset, tagged with the com.ubuntu:type=user user property
Grey (system or persistent, see below for details)
Green (system or persistent, per package hook, see below for details)
Red: Non zfs partition

  • By default, /var is on the system data /
  • We can see 2 different use cases:
  1. The desktop user, who wants to revert the whole system in one global lock, and consider everything outside of /home as system data.
  2. The admin user (typically, server administrator), who wants to persist some parts of the FHS persistent, like logs and some application data like mysql db, jenkins…

For maintenance and coherence reasons, we want to have the same set of datasets on server and desktop. However, default annotations on some of these datasets can vary, and will be differentiated by the installer.

By default:

  • /var is on the system dataset “/” (rpool/ROOT/…)
  • /var/{games,log,mail,spool} are distinct datasets. They will be annotated “com.ubuntu:type=system” on desktop, and will not be annotated on a default server installation. They will be thus rolled back with the system on desktop, and will persist on server, unless the administrator/users changes the annotations. Some UI may help with that process.
  • Some directories like /var/lib/docker, /var/lib/mysql, /var/www are going to be treated on a per-package level. We can introduce a helper in postinst to create a separate dataset (which will mostly be the HOME path of that software). Then, the annotation for those will follow the default annotation of the system (system on desktop, no annotation on server).

This allows administrator to treat /etc as persistent, by creating a separate dataset without com.ubuntu.type:system annotation.

Notes:

  1. Several files outside of the user home directory are owned by the user. In particular files in /var/spool/{anacron,cron,cups,mail}. We can’t create per-file dataset and crontabs/cups/mail seems a corner case enough to consider this as part of the “system” and accepting to lose those changes in case of “system” roll back.        
  2. Some softwares have their own snapshot or handling history mechanism, like Docker or snap. We let them in the “system” zfs dataset at first and can refine later on, as the experimentation goes on.
  3. NFS may need a separate dataset for locks handling. However, it’s not clear yet how we would prevent lock globally, and so, in a first approach will be in the “system” dataset. The reason of excluding the NFS locks directory from the snapshot is to not get old locks when the system is rolled back.

Proposed filesystem hierarchy

vfat/ext4 partitions

  • /boot/grub (MBR / ext4) or /boot/efi partition (EFI / vfat).

ZFS partitions

Pools

2 ZFS pools:

  • bpool: boot pool, without /boot/grub. This one will have basics ZFS settings to be readable by GRUB. This is achieved by disabling all the features with -d then enabling only specific features supported by GRUB.
  • Default options:
  • -o ashift=12 (or more, based on /sys)
  • -d
  • -o feature@async_destroy=enabled
  • -o feature@bookmarks=enabled
  • -o feature@embedded_data=enabled
  • -o feature@empty_bpobj=enabled
  • -o feature@enabled_txg=enabled
  • -o feature@extensible_dataset=enabled
  • -o feature@filesystem_limits=enabled
  • -o feature@hole_birth=enabled
  • -o feature@large_blocks=enabled
  • -o feature@lz4_compress=enabled
  • -o feature@spacemap_histogram=enabled
  • -o feature@userobj_accounting=enabled
  • -o xattr=sa
  • -O compression=lz4
  • -O acltype=posixacl
  • -O relatime=on
  • -O normalization=formD
  • -O mountpoint=/
  • -O canmount=off
  • -O devices=off
  • rpool: root pool; system and user datasets. This one can be encrypted and enable advanced ZFS settings.
  • Default options:
  • -o ashift=12 (or more, based on /sys)
  • -O compression=lz4
  • -O acltype=posixacl
  • -o xattr=sa
  • -O relatime=on
  • -O normalization=formD
  • -O mountpoint=/
  • -O canmount=off
  • -O devices=off
  • -O dnodesize=auto
  • -O sync=disabled  # This could be temporarily set during the install and restore it after but needs to be benchmarked

Containers (canmount=off)

  • rpool/ROOT, contains system datasets present on early boot (before pivot_root). Those could NOT be mounted on top of persistent datasets.
  • rpool/SYSTEM contain system datasets mounted during boot (before local-fs.target). Those could be mounted on top of persistent datasets.
  • rpool/USERDATA, contains “user data” datasets. In this context “user data” means all the non-system data that are potentially owned and managed by a user.
  • bpool/BOOT, contains boot datasets with timestamps for any installed systems.

Datasets

  • bpool/BOOT/:
  • ubuntu_<uuid>: kernels and initrds for ubuntu_<uuid> (“/boot” only, no subdirectories).
  • ubuntu_<uuid>@<timestamp> : snapshot of current “/boot” dataset after installation.

Note that grub will collect all kernels available in every dataset under BOOT/ and  snapshots for any given systems.

  • rpool/ROOT/:
  • ubuntu_<uuid>: “system” / (root)  dataset. Can be activated via grub menu option.
  • ubuntu_<uuid>@<timestamp>: snapshot of ”system” / (root) dataset at <timestamp>
  • optionally:
  • ubuntu_<uuid>/opt: “system” /opt dataset. Activated when ubuntu_uuid is selected in grub.
  • ubuntu_<uuid>/opt@<timestamp>: snapshot of ”system” /opt dataset at <timestamp>.
  • rpool/SYSTEM/:
  • ubuntu_<uuid>: “system” / (root) dataset (not mounted), acting as a container for other system datasets under it. Those are mounted on top of ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>.
  • ubuntu_<uuid>/var/lib/apt: system dataset linked to ubuntu_<uuid> boot. This one can be mounted on top of a persistent /var/lib dataset for instance.
  • ubuntu_<uuid>/var/lib/apt@<timestamp>: snapshot of system dataset linked to ubuntu_<uuid> boot.
  • rpool/USERDATA/:
  • home/<user1>_<uuiduser1>: /home/<user1> dataset, with a <uuiduser1>. Current active dataset as canmount=yes. Inactive dataset have canmount=noauto.
  • uuiduser1 is different than <uuid> system dataset.
  • org.zsys:system-dataset=rpool/ubuntu_<uuid>.
  • org.zsys:system-dataset is empty when it’s the active dataset for a given system.
  • When reverting to a snapshot, a transient property is set to the previous active dataset. This fixes the case of:
  1. We reverted system and user data. Older user data are thus becoming active.
  2. The revert doesn’t boot.
  3. We reboot previous active system dataset. We can thus associate user data with the previous “latest” again.
  • home/<user1>_<uuiduser1>@<timestamp>
  • root_<uuidroot1>
  • root_<uuidroot1>@<timestamp>
  • Optionally persistent dataset:
  • rpool/var/lib: any datasets directly under rpool/ are considered persistent datasets. Only SYSTEM/ datasets can be mounted on top of that one.
  • To minimize the difference between Ubuntu Desktop Server, we will create rpool/var/{lib,games,…) for Ubuntu Server (persistent dataset) and rpool/SYSTEM/ubuntu-<uuid>/var/{lib,games,…} on Ubuntu Desktop.
  • Those don’t have any snapshot or garbage collection facility.
  • Users can move between persistent and system layout via a simple zfs rename.

Notes:

  • Cleanup/Usage tracking:
  • ubuntu_<uuid> datasets have a org.zsys:last_used properties, refreshed when mounted at boot and shutdown times. Those are  used to track regular usage of those datasets.
  • At some point, every ubuntu_<uuid> datasets (and associated snapshots) are destroyed if those are not the currently running datasets, any aren’t manually mounted  and org.zsys:last_used is older than a defined period of time.
  • Similar cleanup logic are used for user datasets.
  • “ubuntu_<uuid>”: uuid is generated at install time for first system dataset. As additional boot environments are created by the CLI tool, it will generate new UUIDs for additional ubuntu_<uuid> datasets.
  • Other system and user datasets uuids are generated in initramfs, when choosing a snapshot to revert from.
  • ubuntu in ubuntu_ is taken from /etc/os-release or can be overridden in our command/daemon tools via an environment variable.
  • This designed naming convention (hardcoding only distro name) avoids having two distro_uuid1_uuid2 (where uuid1 would be fixed by installs), BUT some of the shortcomings is that 2 system installations with the same distribution can’t use the same pool.
  • What about multi-pool detection (like persistent datasets on multiple pool?) Do we only ignore SYSTEM/ ROOT/ from other than current pool?

Timestamps (epoch) are in UTC format before being converted in user-readable data.

Grub

Boot menu

From the grub menu the user must be able to boot:

  1. Current ZFS based Ubuntu release in normal or recovery mode.
  2. Any snapshot or clones or current ZFS based Ubuntu release in normal or recovery mode.
  3. Any non-ZFS based Ubuntu release in normal or recovery mode  installed alongside a ZFS based Ubuntu release.
  4. Any other Linux distribution (with advanced options as submenu)
  5. Any other non-Linux distribution.
  6. Run memory tests.

Example of GRUB boot menu structure

Ubuntu 18.04 LTS
Advanced options for Ubuntu 18.04 LTS
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.15.0-3ubuntu1
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.15.0-3ubuntu1 (recovery mode)
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.12.0-2ubuntu5
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.12.0-2ubuntu5 (recovery mode)

Standard Ubuntu Release or other linuxes (non-ZFS)

Ubuntu 19.10
Advanced options for Ubuntu 19.10
        Ubuntu 19.10, with Linux 4.15.0-3ubuntu1
        Ubuntu 19.10, with Linux 4.15.0-3ubuntu1 (recovery mode)
        Ubuntu 19.10, with Linux 4.12.0-2ubuntu5
        Ubuntu 19.10, with Linux 4.12.0-2ubuntu5 (recovery mode)
Ubuntu 19.10 History
        Revert Ubuntu 19.10 to <snapshot name> | 10/04/2019 at 10:01
                Revert system only
                Revert system and user data
                Revert system only (recovery mode)
                Revert system and user data (recovery mode)
        Revert Ubuntu 19.10 to Ubuntu 19.04 <snapshot name> | 09/04/2019 at 13:37
                Revert system only
                Revert system and user data
                Revert system only (recovery mode)
                Revert system and user data (recovery mode)

ZFS Ubuntu Release (zsys)

Ubuntu 19.10
Advanced options for Ubuntu 19.10
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.15.0-3ubuntu1
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.15.0-3ubuntu1 (recovery mode)
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.12.0-2ubuntu5
        Ubuntu, with Linux 4.12.0-2ubuntu5 (recovery mode)
Ubuntu 19.10 History
        Boot Ubuntu 19.10 on <snapshot name> | 10/04/2019 at 10:01
                One time boot
                One time boot (recovery mode)
                Revert system (all intermediate snapshots will be destroyed)
        Boot Ubuntu 19.10 on Ubuntu 19.04 <snapshot name> | 09/04/2019 at 13:37
                One time boot
                One time boot (recovery mode)
                Revert system (all intermediate snapshots will be destroyed)

ZFS Ubuntu Release (non-zsys)

Memory test (memtest86+)
Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)
System Setup

non-OS

Microsoft Windows 10

Other OS

Boot menu generation

Pseudocode to generate GRUB boot menu:

  • For each pool on the system
  • identify root datasets.
  • For each root dataset
  • Retrieve /etc/machine-id (/!\ etc can be a children datasets, which is fine as long as it’s in ROOT/)
  • Retrieve /etc/os-release (Add support to os-prober?)
  • Retrieve user property org.zsys:last-used from the dataset.
  • Retrieve snapshots from the datasets.
  • Collect all available kernels for this dataset and snapshotted ones. Stop at first not empty:
  • First in /etc/fstab (apart for detecting /etc)
  • Then under <current_dataset_path>/boot (or original_dataset_path>/boot<@snapshot>
  • Then in current pool with <dataset_name>/boot (or dataset_name with mountpoint=/boot) or dataset_name/boot@snapshot
  • Then in any other pool <dataset_name>/boot
  • Then in current pool any /boot mountpoint with canmount=yes
  • Then in any other pool any /boot mountpoint with canmount=yes
  • We use the same logic to detect /etc
  • For each machine-id:
  • If zsys:
  • sort(datasets by last-used or snapshot timestamp) desc order
  • else:
  • datasets = [dataset (first) which has mountpoint != legacy and != none] (not coming from a cloned snapshot) + [snapshots of that dataset, ordered by snapshot name]
  • print(“os-released” os-probed) of datasets[0]
  • rpool=dataset_path
  • print(“os-released” os-probed) of datasets[0]
  • print(History)

Open questions

Offline updates

Livepatch

  1. Every livepatch action needs to snapshot the current “/” dataset. ZFS snapshot daemon  needs to be hooked in.
  2. How do livepatch handles updates? If the generation is done from kernels that are in the distro, then we only apply livepatch on the current “/” dataset. Background offline updates will only fetch new kernels, and so, will have the same security/bug protections.

Can we make the offline update compatible with it?

New package installation

When choosing offline updates mode, it’s explicit for the user that updates will only be available on next reboot.

However, the user will still want to install new snap/apt package and have them available immediately. A snapshot needs to be taken at that time. However, this means installing the new package/snap twice: one in current “/” dataset, and one on the offline update dataset if there is a pending one (new updates waiting to be applied) since last reboot.

Note: we need to annotate “last successful boot” on dataset, as previous “/” isn’t necessarily (livepatch/new packages) the last successful boot content.

Hooks for packages?

Should we offer a way for packages to create zfs dataset and tell if they are “system” or “user data” type. For instance: GDM with user home GDM, docker, …

Importing bpool and filesystems

The boot pool ("bpool") needs to be imported at boot. The current pool importing infrastructure (zfs-import-cache.service and zfs-import-scan.service) needs investigation. zfs-import-scan.service is disabled by default, so a cachefile is required. However, that may be too fragile to rely on. It might be better to use a custom service (or some code in the initramfs) to import bpool.

With the design of /boot being ZFS, separate from /, and /boot/efi and /boot/grub being separate mountpoints, either a manual /etc/fstab for /boot will be required (which complicates the dataset management and rollback scenarios) or we need systemd.mount integration. The latter has been accepted upstream but has not yet been released. It can probably be backported if needed prior to the ZoL 0.8.0 release. See: https://github.com/zfsonlinux/zfs/pull/7329

GRUB

In /etc/grub.d/10_linux, GRUB determines the root pool name as follows:
rpool=`${grub_probe} --device ${GRUB_DEVICE} --target=fs_label 2>/dev/null || true`

This fails when the root pool uses a ZFS pool feature that GRUB does not recognized, as grub-probe returns an error of "unknown filesystem". We will need to modify that to detect the pool in some other way, or change the behavior of grub-probe. Until then, in /etc/default/grub, set:
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="root=ZFS=rpool/ROOT/ubuntu"

WIP:

Other OSes/distros

We need to think about other OSes/distro vs our GRUB changes (what will be presented for Ubuntu with a vanilla GRUB?)

Management UI

For example see snapper from Suse for a snapshot management tool.

Raw performance compared to ext4

Disk space consumption with snapshots and clones

Mail with Colin Ian King

With Didier we are experimenting with ZFS and using it on root on
Desktop. Online information (mainly comments on blog posts) shows
contradictory information regarding performances and we'd like to
measure it ourselves with standard desktop loads.

This indeed a tricky issue as everyone seems to have different personal
optimization tweaks for their hardware.  ZFS really requires a lot of
memory (4GB) to work really well and it's known to be less performance
than ext4 in many ways; ZFS tries harder than ext4 to ensure data is
really consistent, especially for the more complex multi-disk
configurations.

There are many different issues to think about, HDD vs SSD, block size
512 byte vs 4K or higher, ZFS ashift settings, compression, CPU and
memory size + speeds, let alone all the settings for raid stripe widths,
free space impact (less than 10% free space and ZFS performance
degrades), async IO, ARC size, etc.  See
http://open-zfs.org/wiki/Performance_tuning

Hence the "out of the box" defaults are always going to be problematic
as they never match the optimal config for the wide range of use cases
and media being used.

Would you recommend any benchmark tool to perform these tests and
compare zfs tu current ext4 setups?

I always use fio, it's very flexible.  In fact, I have some tests that I
regularly use to benchmark xfs, ext2, ext3, ext4 and btrfs:

https://kernel.ubuntu.com/git/cking/fs-test-proto.git/tree/fio-tests/jobs

Notes

Kernel updates and loading a kernel “in the future”

This is the case where the user reverts to a previous version of the system, then wants to boot to the latest version of the system but the grub configuration has been reverted and doesn’t have any entry for the version in the “future”.

Having an entry is doable (only relying on ZFS snapshots) However, what does grub sees as “/boot” content when after a revert?

Discussion with Richard Laager on April 9th 2019

Comment from Richard

At first glance, I don't love this. It seems to be moving further away from established conventions.

Is the goal here to be able to take and rollback atomically rpool/SYSTEM and rpool/USERDATA?

Separately, I'd prefer shorter names as much as possible. rpoot/USER or rpool/DATA might be better than rpool/USERDATA.

I haven't had a chance to look at it in detail yet, but a user pointed out zedenv, which looks very promising:

I'm hoping to find some time soon to test zedenv & zedenv-grub, with a separate bpool dataset for /boot/grub (bpool/grub or bpool/boot/grub).

In any event, I'd imagine that we're going to need some code in the initramfs script to mount bpool/BOOT/<distro>_<uuid> to /boot, where <distro>_<uuid> corresponds to the rpool/ROOT/<distro>_<uuid> currently mounted at /. One option would be to have the GRUB config generator pass an option (e.g. bootmount=bpool/BOOT/ubuntu_<uuid>) if and only if /boot is a different dataset, and then the initramfs just needs to import that pool and mount it, if and only if that option is present. This would maintain compatibility with existing systems.

Here's how this works on existing systems (e.g. Solaris) with beadm, which is what zedenv also does: You start with a "boot environment" named, in this case, "ubuntu". That would be a dataset named rpool/ROOT/ubuntu. Then, as you make changes to the system (e.g. installing software), you can create additional boot environments. Those would be ubuntu-1, ubuntu-2, etc. Those are literal names. They're short and easy for users to work with, unlike things that involve UUIDs.

These datasets are clones. While clones require an underlying snapshot, of course, the boot environments aren't simply snapshots. Since they aren't just snapshots, but clone datasets, we need a container to put them in. That's what rpool/ROOT is for. We end up with rpool/ROOT/ubuntu, rpool/ROOT/ubuntu-1, rpool/ROOT/ubuntu-2.

There is a concept of an "active on reboot" boot environment. How this is tracked is negotiable. What "active on reboot" means here is that it is the boot environment into which GRUB will boot by default. The proposal here is to list the rest of the boot environments in the GRUB menu, such that the user can switch to a different one (mainly to roll back).

See this:

`beadm list` on Solaris has the same output.

Before deviating from the existing design, which has worked for over a decade on other platforms, we should have a clear idea of a problem with it that we're trying to solve.

From my perspective, the only new problem here is that we need a separate pool for /boot because of GRUB's lack of support for modern OpenZFS features. The proposal here was to have bpool/BOOT/* match rpool/ROOT/*. So when we clone rpool/ROOT/ubuntu into rpool/ROOT/ubuntu-1, we'd also clone bpool/BOOT/ubuntu into bpool/BOOT/ubuntu-1. And on boot, when we mount rpool/ROOT/x, we need to mount the corresponding bpool/BOOT/x at /boot.

The GRUB configuration, in /boot/grub, needs to be separate from /boot, such that it persists even as /boot datasets are rolled back. (In this proposal, you want /boot/grub separate anyway, to keep /boot/grub/grubenv writable.)

1) What are you trying to accomplish with the added complexity of "ubuntu" being named "ubuntu_<uuid>"?

2) If I'm reading this correctly, your ubuntu_<uuid> never changes, as the UUID is generated as part of the initial install. So you're not using <uuid> for each boot environment? Thus there is only one dataset in rpool/ROOT? If so, what's the point of rpool/ROOT in your design?

3) When the user boots into an alternate boot environment, it needs to be writable. This means it cannot be a snapshot, so you need a clone. If they don't exist all the time (because there is just one ubuntu_<uuid>), are envisioning creating them on-demand in the initramfs then? Where are you putting these clones? Is that the reason for rpool/ROOT?

Reply to Richard:

We are actually doing exactly what you tell Solaris was doing, the only "difference" is ubuntu-1 vs ubuntu_<uuid>. So there is no deviation from this design? The goal is to be able to boot multiple systems at each point in time if you select a snapshot and thus do this. Our design relies as well on GRUB listing the active one at first (if we "commit" that the boot was successful after)

Zfs ZOL initramfs script doesn’t handle multiple boot environment. It can boot on a snapshot, which does a clone with ubuntu_timestamp for everything under <pool>/ROOT. We end up with <pool>/ROOT/ubuntu_timestamp, <pool>/ROOT/ubuntu_timestamp/usr (if /usr was a separate datasets) and so on… However, this one is non persistent and recreated if you select back this snapshot in grub at the next boot. We don’t want this, but as well, don’t want to introduce too much difference from upstream ZOL implementation.

Our goal is to have:

  • some datasets that are managed by “zsys” (or named to be defined). A cli tool will help managing them and present those in a user friendly way. So users will never have to deal directly with rpool/ROOT, rpool/SYSTEM or rpool/USERDATA.
  • persistent datasets that are in directly under rpool/
  • we want the boot to be as robust as possible (validating a boot works before “committing it”)

System datasets can be “reverted” or rather clone + boot from selecting a grub options.  User datasets can be kept to head, or, reverted at the same time we boot on a snapshot (more serious case when boot failed). Persistent datasets are never touched.

The goal, from a design perspective, is to clearly identify the different kinds of datasets for people using the “zfs” command. Basically, they don’t handle anything under ROOT/SYSTEM/USERDATA unless they understand the details.

We have a separate rpool/SYSTEM from rpool/ROOT due to the existence of persistence datasets. If you look at our schemas:

  • / is ofc a ROOT system
  • /var/lib is a persistent datasets (see the exchanges we had in previous comments with Steve)
  • /var/lib/apt (and a few others) are system datasets.
  • SYSTEM/ and ROOT/ are linked together.

If we put /var/lib/apt under rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>, those will be mounted by the ZOL initramfs script at boot unconditionally. We thus can’t mount in zfs-mount.service (pre local-fs.target) the persistent /var/lib on top of it, shadowing (if -O is used at mount time) /var/lib/apt, which isn’t what we want.

This is why we separate “datasets needed at boot time” from other system datasets in rpool/SYSTEM. Another way would be to keep them all under ROOT/ and add a special user properties on each child dataset from rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid> to decide if we mount them before pivot_root or not. This is though more deviation from existing ZOL implementation, where our is a 5 line diff.

I think we explained the need of USERDATA (managed especially) and the need to separate them visually from “persistent” datasets.

Let’s go now on the subject of UUID. At install time an ubuntu_<uuid1> is created. This is stable for the time the file system is kept around.

If you revert, selecting a snapshot, we generate a new UUID (for rpool/ROOT and rpool/SYSTEM), then we indeed clone the snapshot. So we end up with ubuntu_<uuid2> (we thus have multiple ubuntu_…) and boot from it. zfs mount -a systemd service will mount the remaining persistent (and stable by name, no zfs rename…), system and home datasets.

if boot is successful, we basically “commit” it by rebuilding grub, putting current ubuntu_uuid2 as the first entry, and ubuntu_uuid1 + all snapshots will be the additional entries.

Note that “ubuntu” is an example, and it could be “<distroname>-<machineid>_<uuid>”. Anyway, the users won’t have to deal with it directly as zsys will abstract it so that multiple systems can be installed side by side.

In this schema, we handle the bpool (BOOT/) as ROOT/ or SYSTEM/ (if we keep SYSTEM and don’t use user properties) in a similar way, in the zfs ZOL initramfs script (that we looked deeply before coming to this design ;))

“Then, as you make changes to the system (e.g. installing software), you can create additional boot environments” -> this would be step2 (what we called semi-offline updates) and we decided on this design for this as well. Revert and “offline updates” are handled the same way (with a clone to ubuntu_<newuuid> and working there, then switching that as active / on reboot).

This design is more robust than the multiple zfs rename of the previous one, and seems actually more in line with what you describe and what Solaris did, which is good to know and validate it. In case of faulty reboot, nothing was changed until we commit it and we can’t lay in an intermediate “wrong” state. I think that answers 1) 2) and 3).

Discussion following this comment/reply

Richard: The UUID vs. incrementing number is one issue. I think the incrementing number is easier for users to manage, but that's a small issue.

Jibel: We tried with incrementing numbers and dates, but as soon as you start rolling back the system several times, the sequences stop being in order which IOHO becomes quickly confusing. That’s the reason why we ended up abstracting completely the identifier by using UUID so the name doesn’t carry any meaning.

Richard: Okay, so that makes sense.

Richard: I suspect you may end up with the same issue in the CLI, though. But I'll defer to someone actually implementing that.

Jibel: The CLI should abstract this with dates, but indeed, we have a little bit more information like “last boot time” and others data which should help.

Richard: Sure, so I'll give up on this issue for now. ;)

Richard: The other issue with the UUID is hopefully something we can address above with more edits. I think we are on the same page for rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>, which is managed by the "beadm" role CLI tool. I'm not sure if I understand the UUID thing for the other datasets, but that's probably because we still aren't on the same page (at a minimum, I am confused) about those datasets, so we should probably sort that out first.

What do you mean by this: "If we put /var/lib/apt under rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>"

Didrocks: The issue is the mount ordering:

  • initramfs mounts / (and other things in rpool/ROOT
  • Then, we have on top of it: (for servers only): /var/lib as a persistent dataset.
  • Richard: This is where I disagree. /var/lib is not a separate (mounted) dataset.
  • On top of it, we have /var/lib/apt which is a system dataset.
  • RIchard: I also disagree here. /var/lib/apt is not a separate dataset. The contents of /var/lib/apt are thus in the root filesystem.
  • Well, here we have a consequence of the above (if /var/lib is a separate dataset, /var/lib/apt needs to be a separate dataset), so both concerned are linked.
  • Richard: Agreed, but that's why you shouldn't do that.

See the discussion with Steve L. The issue is that we can’t create the world at installation time (like /var/lib/docker and every other separate persistent dataset) for every package that requires one in the ubuntu archive. So, there are 2 alternatives:
- /var/lib as a persistent dataset, everything under it will be persistent (apart from some few selected subdirectories when we need to link them to the system state)
- OR per package hook, which is what we proposed at first.

See the various exchanges with Steve, we couldn’t convince him about the per-package hook approach. This is why this we need /var/lib as a persistent datasets. The benefit is that this approach is distro-agnostic.

Richard: Okay. I think you will need per-package hooks anyway, for select packages where that really matters. For example, docker, which manages its own ZFS datasets. Another example might be databases, where you want to set ZFS's recordsize property to match their internal record size, for performance reasons. But I take your point about not wanting to require the use of per-package hooks for everything in /var/lib. I'll think about that some more, but for the sake of discussion now, I'll stipulate that. Then, I agree with your conclusion that /var/lib/apt should be a separate dataset.

Didrocks: Yeah, it’s not obvious at first, but I think for a first experimentation this is OK. The issue is really that in a distro, you don’t really know what ends up on the user system in advance, contrary to a sysadmin. But our discussion raised that we may be able to merge back rpool/ROOT and rpool/SYSTEM by adding user properties (to know what to mount in the initramfs and what to mount in zfs-mount.service). This is a little patch against ZOL initramfs script, but I now think this would be saner in the final implementation.

Richard: So I'm still trying to wrap my head around this, but... So /var/lib becomes a real dataset, which I would place at rpool/var/lib. Then, we create /var/lib/apt, which is now something that is logically linked to rpool/ROOT/ubuntu (ignoring UUID suffixes). So we could place it as rpool/ROOT/ubuntu/var/lib/apt or something, but then, if it is mounted in the initramfs, you end up with the problem you described.

Didrocks: Yeah, let’s ignore UUID for the sake of discussion ;) Right, this was the reason why we separated it at first in the SYSTEM datasets. However, I think we can annotate rpool/ROOT/ubuntu with “org.zsys:boot” for instance (and if you want /opt at early boot for instance, you annotate rpool/ROOT/ubuntu/opt with “org.zsys:boot” and if we find that user annotations in the zfs initramfs script on the system we want to boot, we only mount (as those are manually mounted) those 2 instead of mounting everything under rpool/ROOT/ubuntu. Then, rpool/ROOT/var/lib/apt would be still set as canmount=on, and zfs-mount.service will mount it at the same time that it mounts rpool/var/lib, with the correct order.

Richard: Everything under rpool/ROOT needs to be canmount=noauto, because you are going to have multiples of it. You would end up with multiple copies of /var/lib/apt trying to mount at the same mount point.

Didrocks: Actually, we have a small daemon starting before zfs-mount.service which is going to swap the canmount=noauto and canmount=yes so that only the ones corresponding to the correct “ubuntu_uuid” is mounted by the zfs service. This daemon will be the same that does the second phase commit.

Richard: Sure, that works. Or you could have the initramfs swap them. If something swaps them, it will work. So, what's wrong with this:
rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>
rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>/apt (mountpoint=/var/lib/apt)

Didrocks: Do you think this is better than keeping the fhs layout? I find it a little bit more confusing than have the whole path explicit (especially as we will have multiple datasets under it)

Richard: Well, that wasn't my main question at this point. Historically, I've been the one pushing to keep the ZFS dataset hierarchy matching the filesystem hierarchy, for ease of understanding. If there is more than one, definitely, I'd keep the whole path, even though it looks weird. If this was the one exception, I would be more willing to eat the inconsistency to avoid rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid>/var/lib/apt which is getting pretty long.

Didier: yes, there will be dpkg, aptitude, NetworkManager… So not a single one.

Richard: How many are you thinking in total? Have you considered making a more drastic change? For example, what if you had /var/SOMETHING-ELSE, and moved (with symlinks or not) /var/lib/apt, /var/lib/dpkg, etc. into there. Then /var/SOMETHING-ELSE can naturally just be on /, which keeps this all a lot simpler.

Didier: the issue is that those solutions won’t be distro-agnostic. But yeah, that would have been a lot simpler in the ZFS side. FYI Ubuntu Touch had a lot of those and using as well a farm of bindmounts for RO part of the filesystem and keep it separated from the RW part, and the result was really really hard to manage and quite hackish TBH.

Richard: Is the list of /var/lib/* that needs to be part of the system relatively small, and bounded?

Jibel: So far we’ve identified few of them: apt, dpkg, aptitude, NetworkManager, snapd, AccountServices, experimentation will likely reveal more of them.

Richard: I get the package manager ones and somewhat AccountServices, though I don't really understand that. But why is NetworkManager linked to the system state? If I roll back my root filesystem, why should that roll back my DHCP lease cache or whatever is in there? Looking at it now, I'm not actually sure why it's in /var/lib anyway, rather than /var/cache.

Richard: But anyway, getting back on topic, if the list is small, I suppose this is the right compromise. But I'm not immediately seeing the need for rpool/SYSTEM. It sounds like it's because the current behavior of the initramfs is to mount everything that's under rpool/ROOT/ubuntu, which is problematic here.

Didier: yeah, we didn’t want to change at first the ZOL zfs initramfs script too much. But after our discussion, I think you convinced us to go further and thus, merging rpool/SYSTEM and rpool/ROOT + using zfs properties to decide what to mount before or after pivot_root.

Richard: Is there anything, ever, that needs to be mounted before pivot_root, other than the root filesystem itself? If I had a separate /usr, or something maybe?

Didier: In the default installation, nothing, but if a user wants to have a separate /lib (we don’t have /usr merged in debian/ubuntu), yes this needs to be available at boot, so just after pivot_root, before leaving the initramfs. /usr is required on non debian-based distribution (with systemd).

Richard: Right, okay. So keep the current behavior as the default: anything under the rootfs dataset gets mounted before pivot_root, but a new property (org.zfsonlinux maybe, if you want this merged upstream) skips that. The use case being exactly this, which is that you have data you want to associate with the root filesystem, but it is hierarchically intermixed with non-system data, so you need proper mount ordering.

Didier: yes, that’s our current view on how to approach this. What we will do though is to use another annotation namespace as long as we experiment it before proposing upstream so that we have the design battle tested and not abusing the zol namespace making it people think this is coming and blessed by upstream until it really is :)

Richard: Right, okay, so then rpool/SYSTEM goes away. With that, I'm not seeing a need for rpool/USERDATA. In other words, everything that's not under rpool/ROOT is user data.

Didier: No, everything not under rpool/ROOT is persistent data, not user data.

Richard: What does "user data" mean to you again, as opposed to persistent data?

Didier: “user data” have snapshots, they can be reverted in or not in sync with system data. So, in grub, you have 2 choices:

  • revert to <system> @timestamp
  • revert with user data reverted to <system>@timestamp.

Persistent data are never snapshotted.

Richard: The only user data then is home directories (/home/* and /root)?

Didier: By default, yes, with potentially some other user’s home directory. But also, users can mount extra disk space under /media and they want to have it snapshotted as well, but for some reason, they don’t have them mounted under /home. But in the general case, this is indeed “user’s home”.

Richard: I remain unconvinced by the /media example. That would be a separate pool. (That is another whole discussion: ZFS on removable media). If it's just home directories, then we already have a container for that: rpool/home.

Didrocks: Yes, but that means that it’s not clear to the users and without annotation, it will be hardcoded to have those marked as “user data” and have a specific way to tell “all those datasets can be reverted in sync with a system revert”.

Richard: I'm still not seeing the issue. Removable media, for example, isn't guaranteed to have a matching snapshot to whatever you're reverting to. So this is only an issue for things on the main rpool.

Didier: still, we need a way to identify those special datasets that can be reverted. Hardcoding /home sounds wrong. Adding user properties can create new issues, like “what if a persistent dataset is now within a “user data” datasets. This was our original issue which led us to use UUID instead of a zfs rename party. To be clear: we don’t want people to think they can create a persistent dataset under rpool/home/user/… for instance. This will be unmanaged by the system.

Richard: I'm still not following. Is the issue that you want to allow, for example, /srv to be "user data" that gets rolled back by that second GRUB choice? Or is the issue that you want to prevent someone from doing something?

Didier: There are actually 2 issues:

  • /srv is a good example as you gave (or any company-wide managed HOME directories), and we want them to mark this as “user data”.
  • we want to prevent people from wrongly assuming they can create a persistent dataset in a “user dataset”. However, we still want them to be able to achieve this layout. So let’s say $HOME/snap is a persistent dataset (not defined yet):
  1. You have rpool/home/user1_<uuid1>/ and rpool/home/user1_<uuid1>/snap. First should be rollback, the second is supposedly persistent.
  2. With the separation of user datasets, you would have: rpool/USERDATA/home/user1_<uuid1> and rpool/home/user1/snap.
  3. The rule is also simple for a sysadmin: everything you create directly under rpool/ is a persistent dataset, with no snapshot, nothing.

Richard: So, out of the box, are you still intending that /srv, /var/www, and /var/games be persistent?

Didier: yes, we will create rpool/srv, rpool/var/www, rpool/var/games… (as part of the default layout)

Richard: Okay, so here again, I'll have to think about it, but I think I see your point. I'll stipulate agreement for now. So we have rpool/USERDATA which contains things like home directories, and could, at the admin's option, contain more datasets which are snapshotted and can optionally be rolled back with the root filesystem. Several questions/concerns:

1) I would love to see a shorter name, like rpool/DATA, but maybe I need to get over that.
Didier: I’m more inclined to rpool/USERS for instance than just “DATA” which sounded like too generic (but as a non native speaker). Not decided yet and we can revisit. Let’s go with rpool/USERS for now?
Richard: rpool/DATA is very generic, but if there is (or can be through administrator action) more than just home directories, I'd say that generic-ness is a feature, not a bug. rpool/USERS makes me think it is just home directories, in which case I'd use rpool/HOME (which at least one Gentoo HOWTO uses), but then we could just as well use rpool/home and we're back to the beginning again.

Jibel: So let’s stick with USERDATA for now, which carries the meaning of data belonging to the user but not restricted to home directory (unlike /USERS) as opposed to data belonging to the system.

2) Are you intending on giving a GRUB option to roll back USERDATA without rolling back ROOT?
Didier: No, the options are system only or system + userdata. The option, per-user to rollback its data only will be available in the user session, and will be effective on next login.

3) The above might be dependent on: Is USERDATA snapshotted in tandem with ROOT (e.g. when I do package upgrades), or separately (e.g. every hour)? Or, are you intending on snapshotting the system very frequently too, such that these are the same?

Didier: USERDATA is snapshotted in tandem with ROOT + regularly (but without the system being snaphoted), like every hour with exponential backoff for keeping snapshots alive before they are garbage collected.

4) Do you directly offer me the rollback times in GRUB, or do I get those via some software later in the boot process? If it's in GRUB, that implies that you need to update the GRUB configuration every time you take a snapshot of USERDATA.

Didier: So, we need to rebuild grub in practice (as we don’t offer there rollbacking USERDATA only) everytime we update the system (or install new packages, or after a rollback commit), which sounds OKish. The grub menu will offer the rollback times for the system.

5) If they rollback together, I'm not seeing the association. Is it just time-based?

Didier: The association is done via the @timestamp snapshot, so time-based, indeed. Then, the early boot daemon will do the canmount=on / canmount=noauto dance to mount the correct user data datasets.

Richard: So, related question... why is the daemon doing a canmount dance? Why not just always have them canmount=noauto and have the daemon mount them? Is it because you want systemd or ZFS to deal with mount ordering?

Didier: yes, this sounds more robust, and I also think that having zfs mount -a doing the right thing (if you import the pool on another system with altroot for instance) is a good goal.

Richard: Sure, that's a nice property I hadn't considered. I am concerned about how this will interact with the ZoL systemd mount generator, which is basically just caching the properties into a file. But that's something that can be figured out as implementation is occuring.

Didier: I have already implemented some systemd generator in the past which went in systemd upstream :) So, I think I can have a look, but it sounded like it only created the expected .mount files. I need to check back again on this with ZFS 0.8, but I didn’t look too deeply yet. This is why we want to prototype it.

Richard: So getting back to my previous #5. If I am rolling back to ubuntu_<uuidX>, how does the system know which rpool/USERDATA snapshot to use?

Didier: 2 things: if you are just rolling back, you have in /proc/cmdline rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuidX>@snapshot. So, the early daemon will check for this, find a corresponding @snapshot in every datasets parts of “user data” and will handle the canmount dance before systemd mount them.

Richard: You don't have @snapshot in the cmdline in this case. You have root=rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuidX> and that's it. That said, you can get from the clone to its base snapshot, so that's just one extra indirection. So I think I follow what you'd have to do.

Didier: the initramfs script parse /proc/cmdline and uses @snap though: https://github.com/zfsonlinux/zfs/blob/master/contrib/initramfs/scripts/zfs.in#L911

Also, this is what we looked when doing a rollback test and /proc/cmdline seemed to match that.

Richard: I thought we settled this before. rpool/ROOT/* is a bunch of clones, not snapshots, named ubuntu_<uuid>*. Then the GRUB config has multiple entries, of root=rpoot/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid1>, root=rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuid2>, etc. The @snapshot thing in the initramfs is an alternative design wherein the initramfs clones the snapshot on demand.

Didier: ok, we probably didn’t align on this. So, the idea was to have the @snapshot as designed in the zfs initramfs script.

So you end up, by default with:

  • rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1
  • rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1@timestamp1
  • rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1@timestamp2

        Then,  at some point, you rollback to let’s say @timestamp1

        initramfs creates rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid2, which is a clone of rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1@timestamp1. We thus boot from this one (but we still have in cmdline root=rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1@timestamp1 for this particular boot). As this is the boot we reverted to, the daemon picks that up, does the correct handling of userdata (canmount=on on @timestamp1), creating as well rpool/USERDATA/home_uuid3/ for instance. When boot fininishes up, if successful, we “commit” it by promoting rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid2 and rebuild grub.

On next reboot, we have root=rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid2. We don’t change anything in the early daemon for user datasets as correct canmount=on is already set.

After a while (multiple reboots, some X days passed), if rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1 isn’t used, this one is garbage collected (and same for USERDATA datasets not in used).

So grub lists:

  • every snapshot available on rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_*
  • Richard: Are you sure about that? I think you mean "every snapshot available on the current rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<uuidX>".
  • Didier: why not other snapshots in a “divergent reality? ;)” which are the one in rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_<older_uuid>? Too confusing you think?
  • Richard: Yes, I think this is all a terribly confusing idea. A lot depends on what your snapshot script does. But, for example, if your snapshot script just acts on rpool/ROOT recursively, or on rpool/ROOT/*, which is the same effect, you're going to get new @timestamp3 on both rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid1 and rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid2.
  • Didier: ah no, we snapshot only current “running” system. So for instance zfs snap -r rpool/ROOT/ubutu_uuid2@timestamp. The idea to show in grub timestamps for ubuntu_uuid1 as well is if you want to try doing a bisect. Like “my system worked weeks ago, but I didn’t reboot since then”. I have 4 snapshots
  • -> you rollback to a snapshot from a week ago: OK
  • -> you then rollback to a snapshot which was taken 3 days ago: KO
  • -> and so on, until you spot the system snapshots where it started to fail…
  • Richard: When you zfs promote rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_uuid2, those snapshots are now associated with ubuntu_uuid2, not ubuntu_uuid1.
  • Didier: not every one of them, right? Only those that were taken before the snapshot you revert at. So ubuntu_uuid2 contains all snapshots before @timestamp, and ubuntu_uuid have snapshots attached to it which were “in the future” compared to @timestamp.
  • Richard: That is correct, yes. So you enumerate all these snapshots and presumably sort them by time for the GRUB menu, such that the user doesn't really care which UUID they're on.
  • Didier: correct, the goal is really to make those UUID implementation details that the user shouldn’t care (and even most of them won’t see it)
  • Richard: So again, how is this better than the alternative: Every time you take a snapshot @timestamp123 of rpool/ROOT/ubuntu, you create a clone rpool/ROOT/ubuntu-123.
  • Didier: What’s the benefit of this model?
  • Richard: I don't have a bunch of UUIDs to deal with? Everything Just Makes Sense if I run `zfs list`. There is no need to rely on the initramfs to clone my snapshot on demand. There is no concern about that code path having a destroy in it (which it does). You don't need to add the UUID thing to that codepath in the initramfs. Aside from being time-based, this all works the way Solaris did for 15 years or something. (And my follow up question would again be why it can't be an incrementing number, but I think the answer to that is because of the multiple rollbacks. But time-based doesn't have that problem.)
  • [jibel] time based have the problem that the data can be “in the future” compared to the timestamp.
  • Richard: As in, I rollback to the snapshot named "yesterday at noon". Then I have the data from yesterday at noon. Then I run for a while, and now it's no longer "yesterday at noon"'s data?
  • Didier: let’s take an example. Current is ubuntu-1234 (let’s take a number, but imagine this is a timestamp). Now, with your model, we will continue being on ubuntu-1234 but take regular snapshots creating clones. We thus have after a little while ubuntu-1500, then ubuntu-1900 and so on, are OLDER than ubuntu-1234 which is always “current”. This sounds confusing for the zfs user which may think she/he’s not on the latest.
  • Richard: So I'm almost ready to agree, but... in either case, don't we have a concept of the current dataset, which is displayed differently (e.g. first) in GRUB?
  • Didier: this info will be given to grub when we rebuild it to know which one is either current or “next current”.
  • Richard: So, for example, if I am running in ubuntu_<uuidX>, that will be the first choice.
  • Didier: yes, until we implement semi-offline updates (which will give an argument to next default “ubuntu_<uuidY>”)
  • Richard: Okay, again I'll have to think about this, but let's wind the stack up. So you're going to have snapshots which are time-based, and UUIDs which are the clones that were previously booted into. The time-based options will clearly be unambiguous, as the snapshots are immutable. So this branch seems fine. And I understand now why you're enumerating snapshots across all of rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_*. And since every @timestamp in ROOT will (absent someone deleting them under you) match a snapshot in USERDATA, that's how you you know what to roll back USERDATA to. Okay, that all makes sense...
  • every rpool/ROOT/ubuntu_*
  • Richard: But then how do these show up in GRUB? I get the way you'd show the current one. But how do you show non-current ubuntu_<uuid> clones in GRUB?
  • Didier: We tested in our daemon to create a user properties (org.zsys:last-used) which is a timestamp set at boot time and shutdown times. This is how we know that this datasets has been active. We want to use this for garbage collection “this dataset has not been used for x… and we use the new one for …) and as well for ordering grub. The user will thus not know if he’s booting from a snapshot or an already cloned datasets (coming from a previous rollback). We prevent zfs destroy in initramfs if this property was set.
  • Richard: So you name these based on the timestamp in the user property on the clone itself. That makes sense.

Richard: So I think I'm out of questions on this for now. It seems like you've got a fully-baked solution. I don't know that I love all the trade-offs (e.g. having a bunch of /var/lib/* datasets), but I see how the various factors have resulted in that. I'll give this some more thought, but tentatively, I think it makes sense, with the idea of merging rpool/SYSTEM back into rpool/ROOT.

I think you've considered the tradeoffs in having the naked "ubuntu" name. It doesn't lend itself well to installing a new version of the distro alongside. Obviously, the UUID part works fine, but how you differentiate them in GRUB is a little tricky. I suppose maybe you can just pull out the distro release from inside the dataset. You're probably needing to chroot into them anyway, to know which kernels are available.

Didier: first, thanks for chatting! This is way more effective than comments (I think we would have been at the same point in a year) and happy that you have been able to see we gave that some thoughts, while trying to have as few differences as possible to ZOL. We want, once the experimentation is done (so there is surely room for improvements) merge back what can be merged upstream if the design is fine. I think as well that merging rpool/SYSTEM and rpool/ROOT is a good outcome of this.

We have thought about the naked “ubuntu” name (I guess we even mentioned in the Notes that ubuntu_machineid_uuid would be something) but it makes the names even longer. The issue will be about the garbage collection, so we may need to annotate the machine ID anyway, but not in the name itself. I agree that we will need to scan the datasets themselves for rebuilding grub, and so, maybe, we should just look at /etc/os-release for each of them.

Richard: On other topics, I wanted to draw you attention to this, which may argue against the idea of putting /boot/grub into the ESP: https://utcc.utoronto.ca/~cks/space/blog/linux/UEFIPartitionCorruption

Didier: I’ll give it a look! I’m not a fan either, sounds like it’s one of Steve’s points. This is quite orthogonal to our discussion and can stay on a separate partition for prototyping anyway.

Richard: I also had an idea for how GRUB might be able to write /boot/grub/grubenv. That file has some interesting properties: 1) We can assume a full-file overwrite. 2) The file is small, less than the maximum historic recordsize of 128KiB. 3) GRUB is only writing when the pool is exported.

Richard: As a result, I think we may be able to safely and simply (two key properties for GRUB) write that file by not actually writing it to the pool itself, but only writing it to the ZIL. Then, when the pool is imported normally, the ZIL will be replayed and that change will be merged into the normal pool. Things that read /boot/grub/grubenv from the OS will thus see the change. If it is important for GRUB to see its own changes to grubenv without an intervening OS boot (which it might be, for things like recordfail), then GRUB would also need to be enhanced to read the ZIL, at least in this case.

Didier: interesting approach. I think we need to think as well a little bit more around this, maybe even contacting grub upstream to think what they think about it (we don’t want to diverge too much in those options). But probably something doable. I’ll keep that separated from the zfs prototype anyway for now (dealing with rpool/bpool is already something ;))

Richard: My thought was to discuss this with someone who actually understand the ZFS on-disk format. Then, assuming the idea is solid, GRUB upstream would be a good next step. They may not actually care that much, as long as the patch is clean and works. I'm not necessarily in a position to implement it personally, but it's something to consider. If GRUB could write to /boot/grub/grubenv, then there's really nothing holding back putting /boot/grub into the bpool.

Didier: indeed, and ack. I think I need to read again about it and have some sleeps on it to have a first good answer. I wouldn’t put priority into this personally but will tackle this (anyway we need to rediscuss with Steve again) about the whole boot idea. There is a Canonical sprint in Lyon in a little bit more than a month, which may as well make Steve’s time a little bit more compatible with yours, maybe that would be the correct time for another written chat? Ensuring that we read/advanced as much as possible to see what we can do there?

Richard: Yes, that might work.

Didier: Thanks again for this unusual form of chat application ;) But I guess that will help us as well anyway in detailing/blogging about the desired solution and the tradeoffs involved in them.

Richard: I've been trying to move the upstream HOWTO in the direction of what was discussed here, but I think I'll wait on these changes until you're closer to production. The previous round of changes were pretty minimal, but this is more extensive, even without actually having any snapshots or clones. And I suppose, likewise, I'll hold off investigating zedenv any further. If you're just going to write your own thing anyway, any time I put into zedenv research is wasted.

Didier: makes sense. We had a look at zedenv, but it didn’t seem it would cover some of the needs described above ^ which is why I think building a small system, as robust as possible, would probably be a better investment for us. We want to be sure that it’s easily consumable for other distros still and as close to your HOWTO and zol as possible. But for now, yeah, don’t update it until we have at least the first prototype (which will be really rudimentary) out. We discovered the recent tradeoffs by iterating and imaging some scenarios and trying with a simple shell script, this really helped :)

Richard: One other issue that I've run into is that `zpool status` wants to break your bpool, by suggesting a `zpool upgrade` to enable new features. There has been talk upstream (not just ZoL, but OpenZFS as a whole) about creating a -o features=portable (note features plural, so this is not a feature name). This would be a "set" of features, the definition of which would change over time. I don't particularly care about that part of it, as that is about ensuring your pool is portable to other operating systems (besides just Linux even). But the same infrastructure for specifying a set of features could easily be extended to -o features=grub. This would keep `zpool status` from suggesting a `zpool upgrade` of the bpool. Hopefully this gets written soon, as you really don't want to deploy something in the distro that has that sharp of an edge.

Didier: clearly! Thanks for the notice on that one. I think indeed that some users may experiment with it and run what the command tells them to run. Let’s hope this goes in 0.8 or very shortly post-0.8 so that we can backport it. I’ll try to find a place for those kinds of advice to “things we need to look for”. I think this particular zfs system we are building will be kept as an experimentation at least to the next LTS (I’m not really eager for people just using a checkbox in 20.04 thinking this is stable and battle-tested), but still offering that to people who want to give it a try. So it seems the timeline shouldn’t be a concern if this is already discussed upstream and we need to document caveats like those to make people experimenting it aware until then.

Richard: Worst case, if you need a work-around temporarily, you could just special case the bpool in zpool status & upgrade by name, or by checking a user property.

Didier: yes, this should be easily doable and we can distro-patch this.

Richard: Well, thanks for your time. I will think about all this, but I'm probably more-or-less "stuck" at this point until you have something more developed. I'll try to focus my efforts on the features=grub thing and/or GRUB writing grubenv, to the extent I can/have time.

Didier: thanks again for all those feedbacks! This really helped and was nice to chat live with you directly. As soon as we have something more advanced, we will ping you to have your early points or if we need to change whatever in the design for $REASONS, we’ll ping you back on the document as well. But thanks for everything, you gave us a very good startup point via the HOWTO or just commenting. Keep us posted if you need help in the features=grub and grubenv. I’ll keep you posted about Lyon if we can setup some kinds of meetings.

Richard: Also, you might want to think about what your official public stance is here... I see this has "Strictly confidential" in the footer. The OpenZFS leadership meeting minutes (I didn't attend the meeting) said something like, "We've heard Canonical is working on ..., but we don't know the status of that."

Didier: Thanks for the warning! This is clearly not strictly confidential (it’s coming from the default template, I’m going to remove it). The idea is only to come with a design that we know will work out instead of just talking without having a playing ground in theory. But we’ll give more technical details once we have the first version of a prototype out, which shouldn’t be too long. We need to release 19.04 first though :p

We didn’t share it at large yet because as you saw… the design changed, so no need to confuse people with things that won’t work out ;)

Richard: I believe the current status is: "Canonical is considering root-on-ZFS support." Is that accurate? (You haven't committed yet.) And is that public?

Didier: that’s accurate. However, it won’t be the default anytime soon. It will be an install option, as an experiment.

Richard: Is that public?

Didier: sure, it’s public that we are working on this (we already posted it in the community hub in our weekly report)

Richard: Okay. Yeah, this isn't really anything more than what I read which lead me to contact you. :) Alright, well I probably need to call it a night.

Didier: yes, it’s quite late for you, thanks for taking the time ;) Have a good night and let’s keep in touch.

2019-04-17 Richard:  I discussed the grubenv thing with Richard Yao (who knows far more about ZFS on-disk than me) today. He wasn't 100% sure, but we're leaning towards the idea that it would be easier to get GRUB to write to a "dump device"-style volume than a file. I verified that grub-editenv works fine if given a block device (or symlink to one). So the user-space setup would look like this:
        zfs create -V 1024B -b 1024B -o checksum=noparity bpool/grubenv
        ln -s /dev/zvol/bpool/grubenv /boot/grub/grubenv
        grub-editenv /boot/grub/grubenv create
Then we would need to enhance GRUB to:
        1) Read that symlink. This may already be supported. I haven't tested.
        2) Realize it symlinks to /dev/zvol, which means it needs to access a zvol named bpool/grubenv.
        3) Read from the zvol
        4) Write to the zvol. The key thing here is that the zvol contents can be overwritten in place without the need to update any ZFS metadata, because this has checksum=noparity (which also implies checksum=off). If GRUB can handle calculating raidz parity, then we can reduce this to checksum=off. Since the blocksize is set to 1024B, the data will be logically contiguous. Note that bpool/grubenv must not be snapshotted; as data is being overwritten, snapshots would not work.

0.8 features (https://github.com/zfsonlinux/zfs/releases):

pool trimming

zpool initialize on vm?

item.title: Postgresql when it's not your job
item.description: Comments

item.title: Using pseudo-terminals to control interactive programs, pty, pdip
item.description:
Author: R. Koucha
Last update: 16-Apr-2014


Back to home page
Back to previous page

Using pseudo-terminals (pty) to control interactive programs

Resources
About the author

Foreword


A french version of this article has been published in
special issue 34.

Introduction

Although less and less popular, the interactive applications in command line mode interacting with an operator through a terminal on a serial port, are still widely used. Especially in the embedded world where the graphical resources are superfluous or too expensive. Among those applications, we can quote as examples:
  • bash: the default Linux shell
  • bc: the calculator
  • ftp: file transfer utility
  • telnet: remote terminal
It is possible to use those tools in shell scripts in order to automate some tasks like tests or system maintenance and administration. For example, we could use telnet in a script to connect to a remote machine to trigger some operations. Unfortunately, this is not that simple because an interactive program needs a human to operate.

This article focuses on a solution based on pseudo-terminals to automate interactive programs.


Redirection of the standard input and outputs of a process

Once a program is loaded into memory to be executed, it becomes a process attached to the current terminal. By default, the standard input (stdin) comes from the  keyboard and the standard outputs (stdout and stderr) are redirected to the screen (cf. figure 1).
Figure 1: Standard input and outputs of a Linux process

Linux provides the ability to redirect the standard input and outputs in order to get data from another source than the keyboard and display data to another destination than the screen. This feature is very powerful: a process reads its standard input and writes to its standard outputs without knowing any details about the devices. In other words, a program can run without any modifications to read from the keyboard or a file or the output of another process (pipe mechanism). It is the same for the outputs.
Let's consider the following program called mylogin which gets a login name and a password:

#include <stdio.h>

int main(void)
{
char login_name[150];
char password[150];

// By default stdin, stdout and stderr are open

fprintf(stdout, "Login : ");
if (NULL == fgets(login_name, sizeof(login_name), stdin))
{
fprintf(stderr, "No login name\n");
return 1;
}

fprintf(stdout, "Password : ");
if (NULL == fgets(password, sizeof(password), stdin))
{
fprintf(stderr, "No password\n");
return 1;
}

fprintf(stdout, "Result :\n%s%s\n", login_name, password);

return 0;
}

Under a shell like bash, multiple solutions are available to make redirections. If we launch the program as it is, the input is the keyboard and the outputs are the screen.

$ ./mylogin
Login : bar
Password : foo
Result :
bar
foo
$

The preceding program can be launched as follow to redirect the output to the file output.txt.
$ ./mylogin > output.txt
bar
foo
$ cat output.txt
Login : Password : Result :
bar
foo
$

We can see that without any modifications in the program mylogin, it has been possible to launch it to have the standard output redirected to the screen and then to the file output.txt.

Problems while automating interactive programs

A program as simple as mylogin can be automated. That is to say that the human operator can be replaced by a program like a shell script. Let's consider the file input.txt into which we have stored the answers expected by mylogin.

$ cat input.txt
bar
foo
$

We can launch mylogin with input.txt as standard input:

$ ./mylogin < input.txt
Login : Password : Result :
bar
foo
$

So, we have replaced the human operator by a file containing the expected entries. Unfortunately, it is not possible to apply this method to all interactive programs. Some of them are very elaborated. A program reading a password typically flushes its standard input right after having displayed the password prompt to get rid of any characters entered between the login name and the password prompt (the character echoing is also deactivated during the password entry). We can illustrate this by making mylogin flush its standard input just before the password entry (call to fseek()).

#include <stdio.h>

int main(void)
{
char login_name[150];
char password[150];

// By default stdin, stdout and stderr are open

fprintf(stdout, "Login : ");
if (NULL == fgets(login_name, sizeof(login_name), stdin))
{
fprintf(stderr, "No login name\n");
return 1;
}

// Flush standard input
fseek(stdin, 0, SEEK_END);

fprintf(stdout, "Password : ");
if (NULL == fgets(password, sizeof(password), stdin))
{
fprintf(stderr, "No password\n");
return 1;
}

fprintf(stdout, "Result :\n%s%s\n", login_name, password);

return 0;
}

When interacting with the operator, the program behaves the same (the keyboard is the standard input).

$ ./mylogin
login : bar
Password : foo
La saisie est :
bar
foo

On the other side, when the standard input is a file, the error message "No password" is displayed. Actually, the second call to fread() gets an end of file which means that there are no more data on input.

$ ./mylogin < input.txt
No password
Login : Password :
$

When the operator interacts, he waits for the display of the password prompt before entering the password. When the entry is the file input.txt, the entired file is entered at the beginning of the program. So, the first line is read with the first call to fread() and the second line is flushed by the call to fseek(). That's why the second fread() encounters an end of file. This is a typical case where the program is desynchronized with its standard input.
From this example, we encountered one of the numerous problem we can have while attempting to automate an interactive program. These kind of programs also suppose that their standard input and output are terminals. So, they can trigger some terminal specific operations like "echo off", "canonical mode", "line mode"... If the input and output are not terminals but files for example, these operations will fail and trigger errors in the program.
The pseudo-terminal concept is a solution to those problems.

Introduction to pseudo-terminals

A pseudo-terminal is a pair of character mode devices also called pty. One is master and the other is slave and they are connected with a bidirectional channel. Any data written on the slave side is forwarded to the output of the master side. Conversely, any data written on the master side is forwarded to the output of the slave side as depicted in figure 2.

Figure 2: Overview of a pseudo-terminal

The slave side behaves exactly as a standard terminal as any process can open it to make it its standard input and outputs. So, all the operations like disabling the echo, setting the line mode or canonical mode are available.
The master side is not a terminal. It is just a device which permits to send/receive data to/from the slave side.
In the Unix world, there are multiple implementation of the pseudo-terminals. There are the BSD and the System V versions. The Linux world recommends the system V implementation also called "Unix 98 pty". This is the one we are going to study below.

API of the pseudo-terminals

The API is quite simple:
  • posix_openpt()
    This call creates the master side of the pty. It opens the device /dev/ptmx to get the file descriptor belonging to the master side.
  • grantpt()
    The file descriptor got from posix_openpt() is passed to grantpt() to change the access rights on the slave side: the user identifier of the device is set to the user identifier of the calling process. The group is set to an unspecified value (e.g. "tty") and the access rights are set to crx--w----.
  • unlockpt()
    After grantpt(), the file descriptor is passed to unlockpt() to unlock the slave side.
  • ptsname()
    In order to be able to open the slave side, we need to get its file name through ptsname().
To this API, we can mention the standard API of the terminals: tcgetattr(), cfmakeraw()...

The following program called mypty uses the API to create a pseudo-terminal.
#define _XOPEN_SOURCE 600
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <errno.h>

int main(void)
{
int fdm;
int rc;

// Display /dev/pts
system("ls -l /dev/pts");

fdm = posix_openpt(O_RDWR);
if (fdm < 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on posix_openpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

rc = grantpt(fdm);
if (rc != 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on grantpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

rc = unlockpt(fdm);
if (rc != 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on unlockpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

// Display the changes in /dev/pts
system("ls -l /dev/pts");

printf("The slave side is named : %s\n", ptsname(fdm));

return 0;
} // main

The programs lists the content of the directory /dev/pts at the beginning and at the end to show the creation of the slave side. In the following example, this is the slave number 4 which is created:

$ ./mypty
total 0
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 0 2007-09-25 13:56 0
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 1 2007-09-25 13:32 1
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 2 2007-09-25 12:58 2
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 3 2007-09-25 07:32 3
total 0
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 0 2007-09-25 13:56 0
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 1 2007-09-25 13:32 1
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 2 2007-09-25 12:58 2
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 3 2007-09-25 07:32 3
crw--w---- 1 koucha tty 136, 4 2007-09-25 13:56 4
The slave side is named : /dev/pts/4
$

Usage of the pseudo-terminals

A pseudo-terminal is mainly used to make a process believe that it interacts with a terminal although it actually interacts with one or more processes.

Inter-process communication through a pseudo-terminal

To point out the pseudo-terminal functions, we can modify mypty into mypty2.

#define _XOPEN_SOURCE 600
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#define __USE_BSD
#include <termios.h>


int main(void)
{
int fdm, fds, rc;
char input[150];

fdm = posix_openpt(O_RDWR);
if (fdm < 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on posix_openpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

rc = grantpt(fdm);
if (rc != 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on grantpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

rc = unlockpt(fdm);
if (rc != 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on unlockpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

// Open the slave PTY
fds = open(ptsname(fdm), O_RDWR);

// Creation of a child process
if (fork())
{
  // Father
 
  // Close the slave side of the PTY
  close(fds);
  while (1)
  {
    // Operator's entry (standard input = terminal)
    write(1, "Input : ", sizeof("Input : "));
    rc = read(0, input, sizeof(input));
    if (rc > 0)
    {
      // Send the input to the child process through the PTY
      write(fdm, input, rc);

      // Get the child's answer through the PTY
      rc = read(fdm, input, sizeof(input) - 1);
      if (rc > 0)
      {
        // Make the answer NUL terminated to display it as a string
        input[rc] = '\0';

        fprintf(stderr, "%s", input);
      }
      else
      {
        break;
      }
    }
    else
    {
      break;
    }
  } // End while
}
else
{
struct termios slave_orig_term_settings; // Saved terminal settings
struct termios new_term_settings; // Current terminal settings

  // Child

  // Close the master side of the PTY
  close(fdm);

  // Save the default parameters of the slave side of the PTY
  rc = tcgetattr(fds, &slave_orig_term_settings);

  // Set raw mode on the slave side of the PTY
  new_term_settings = slave_orig_term_settings;
  cfmakeraw (&new_term_settings);
  tcsetattr (fds, TCSANOW, &new_term_settings);

  // The slave side of the PTY becomes the standard input and outputs of the child process
  close(0); // Close standard input (current terminal)
  close(1); // Close standard output (current terminal)
  close(2); // Close standard error (current terminal)

  dup(fds); // PTY becomes standard input (0)
  dup(fds); // PTY becomes standard output (1)
  dup(fds); // PTY becomes standard error (2)

  while (1)
  {
    rc = read(fds, input, sizeof(input) - 1);

    if (rc > 0)
    {
      // Replace the terminating \n by a NUL to display it as a string
      input[rc - 1] = '\0';

      printf("Child received : '%s'\n", input);
    }
    else
    {
      break;
    }
  } // End while
}

return 0;
} // main


The program consists of two processes. The father reads a string from the keyboard and writes it on the master side of the pty. The child replaced its standard input and outputs by the slave side of the pty. It reads the slave side and displays what it received on the slave side prefixed by "Child received : ". Here is an example of execution:

$ ./mypty2
Input : azerty
Child received : 'azerty'
Input : qwerty
Child received : 'qwerty'
Input : pwd
Child received : 'pwd'
Figure 3 depicts the behaviour of the program when the operator enters "qwerty".



Figure 3: Description of mypty2

On the slave side we can note the calls to cfmakeraw() and tcsetattr() to reconfigure the slave side of the pty. This sets the raw mode to disable the echoing among other things.

We can make mypty2 more generic in order to be able to execute any program behind the pty (slave side). In mypty3, the father process writes all the data from its standard input to the master side of the pty and writes all the data from the master side of the pty to its standard output. The child process behaves the same as in mypty2 but executes an interactive program along with its parameters passed as arguments to the program. We can note the calls to setsid() and ioctl(TIOCSCTTY) to make the pty be the control terminal of the executed program. We can also note the closing of the fds file descriptor which becomes useless after the calls to dup().

#define _XOPEN_SOURCE 600
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#define __USE_BSD
#include <termios.h>
#include <sys/select.h>
#include <sys/ioctl.h>
#include <string.h>


int main(int ac, char *av[])
{
int fdm, fds;
int rc;
char input[150];

// Check arguments
if (ac <= 1)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s program_name [parameters]\n", av[0]);
exit(1);
}

fdm = posix_openpt(O_RDWR);
if (fdm < 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on posix_openpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

rc = grantpt(fdm);
if (rc != 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on grantpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

rc = unlockpt(fdm);
if (rc != 0)
{
fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on unlockpt()\n", errno);
return 1;
}

// Open the slave side ot the PTY
fds = open(ptsname(fdm), O_RDWR);

// Create the child process
if (fork())
{
fd_set fd_in;

  // FATHER

  // Close the slave side of the PTY
  close(fds);

  while (1)
  {
    // Wait for data from standard input and master side of PTY
    FD_ZERO(&fd_in);
    FD_SET(0, &fd_in);
    FD_SET(fdm, &fd_in);

    rc = select(fdm + 1, &fd_in, NULL, NULL, NULL);
    switch(rc)
    {
      case -1 : fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on select()\n", errno);
                exit(1);

      default :
      {
        // If data on standard input
        if (FD_ISSET(0, &fd_in))
        {
          rc = read(0, input, sizeof(input));
          if (rc > 0)
          {
            // Send data on the master side of PTY
            write(fdm, input, rc);
          }
          else
          {
            if (rc < 0)
            {
              fprintf(stderr, "Error %d on read standard input\n", errno);
              exit(1);
            }
          }
        }

        // If data on master side of PTY
        if (FD_ISSET(fdm, &fd_in))
        {
          rc = read(fdm, input, sizeof(input));
          if (rc > 0)
          {
